We ought to value the privileges of God's people, both for ourselves and for our children, above all other advantages. No personal blemishes, no crimes of our forefathers, no difference of nation prevents anyone from receiving blessings under the Christian dispensation. But an unsound heart will deprive us of blessings; and a bad example, or an unsuitable marriage, may prevent our children from receiving them.
The camp of the Lord must have nothing offensive in it. If such care is taken to preserve the body clean, much more should we be careful to keep the mind pure.

It is honorable to shelter and protect the weak, provided they are not wicked. New converts to the truth should be treated with particular tenderness, so that they may have no temptation to return to the world. We cannot honor God with our possessions unless they have been honestly and honorably obtained. It must not only be considered what we give, but how we got it. Where the borrower gets, or hopes to get, something of value, the lender should share in the gain; but pity must be shown to him who borrows for necessary food. Any solemn and deliberate vow that comes out of your mouth must not be recalled, but you should keep and perform it punctually and fully. They were allowed to pluck and eat of the corn or grapes that grew by the roadside; only they must not carry any away. This law indicated what great abundance of corn and wine they would have in Canaan. It provided for the support of poor travelers, and teaches us to be kind to such people, teaches us to be ready to distribute, and not to think everything lost that is given away. Yet it forbids us to abuse the kindness of friends, or to take advantage of what is allowed. Faithfulness to their commitments should mark the people of God; and they should never encroach upon others.
